coredata.dc:title: One-pot preparation of nitrogen-doped carbon dots for sensitive and selective detection of Ag+ and glutathione
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Xu
Bai, Jianliang
Yuan, Guojun
Zhang, Lu
Ren, Lili
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a novel nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) nanosensor for detecting Ag+ and glutathione (GSH) has been designed. Interestingly, Ag+ could significantly improve the fluorescence (FL) intensity of the N-CDs we prepared, which is different from most other metal ions with FL quenching effects on N-CDs. The sensing mechanism can be attributed to the oxidation characteristics of Ag+ and the formation of silver nanoparticles (AgNPs). In the reaction, the N-CDs acted as the reducing agent and stabilizer. Under the optimal conditions, the N-CDs could detect Ag+ with a nice linear relationship of R2 = 0.992 and the limit of detection was 0.091 μM. Furthermore, the FL of N-CDs solutions could return to nearly original intensity after introducing glutathione (GSH), which allowed the N-CDs to be applied for GSH detection sensitively and selectively. In addition, the N-CDs were also successfully applied to cellular multicolor imaging of Hela cells. The results reported here pave the way for N-CDs-based fluorescent “off-on-off” sensor in environment, bioassay and bioimaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Fluorescence
Silver ion detection
GSH detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence measurements for Ag+ and GSH
Synthesis and characterization of the N-CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by A Project Subsidized the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (1107047002).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of N-CDs. (b) Particle size distribution analysis of N-CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CDs solution, insets showed the photographs of N-CDs taken under visible light and UV light. (b) The fluorescent spectra of N-CDs solution at different excitation wavelengths.

(a) The changes of FL spectra of N-CDs-Ag+ mixture with increasing concentrations of Ag+ (0–70 μM) under excitation wavelength of 400 nm. (b) The relationship between FL intensity change (I0-I)/I0 and Ag+ concentration (0–70 μM). (c) Linear plots of FL intensity change (I0-I)/I0 as a function of the Ag+ concentration. (d) Selectivity of N-CDs towards Ag+ over various other metal ions. The concentration of each cation is 100 μM. I0 and I are the FL intensities of N-CDs-Ag+ mixture in the absence and presence of Ag+, respectively.

(a) The changes of FL spectra of N-CDs/Ag+/GSH system with GSH concentration from 0 μM to 100 μM. (b) The relationship between FL intensity change (I0-I)/I0 and GSH concentration (0–100 μM). (c) Linear plots of FL intensity change (I0-I)/I0 as a function of the GSH concentration. (d) Selectivity of N-CDs/Ag+ towards GSH over different materials. The concentration of different materials is 150 μM. I0 and I are the FL intensities of N-CDs/Ag+/GSH system in the absence and presence of GSH, respectively.

(a) Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of AgNPs which were synthesized during the reaction progress of Ag+ (70 μM) and N-CDs. (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CDs, Ag+ (70 μM), and GSH (100 μM) system. (c) Schematic of the fluorescence reaction of N-CDs, Ag+ and GSH.

Confocal fluorescence images of hela cells incubated with 50 μg/mL N-CDs at 37 °C for 1.5 h, with bright field (a), excitation wavelength of 410 nm (b), 500 nm (c), 600 nm (d) and overlap field (e-g), respectively.

head.ce:title: One-pot preparation of nitrogen-doped carbon dots for sensitive and selective detection of Ag+ and glutathione

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Southeast University, Nanjing 211189, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Southeast University, Nanjing 211189, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a novel nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) nanosensor for detecting Ag+ and glutathione (GSH) has been designed. Interestingly, Ag+ could significantly improve the fluorescence (FL) intensity of the N-CDs we prepared, which is different from most other metal ions with FL quenching effects on N-CDs. The sensing mechanism can be attributed to the oxidation characteristics of Ag+ and the formation of silver nanoparticles (AgNPs). In the reaction, the N-CDs acted as the reducing agent and stabilizer. Under the optimal conditions, the N-CDs could detect Ag+ with a nice linear relationship of R2 = 0.992 and the limit of detection was 0.091 μM. Furthermore, the FL of N-CDs solutions could return to nearly original intensity after introducing glutathione (GSH), which allowed the N-CDs to be applied for GSH detection sensitively and selectively. In addition, the N-CDs were also successfully applied to cellular multicolor imaging of Hela cells. The results reported here pave the way for N-CDs-based fluorescent “off-on-off” sensor in environment, bioassay and bioimaging.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid development of industry, water pollution by heavy metal ions has been a serious threat to organisms and environment and becomes an extremely tricky problem [1–4]. As one of the heavy metal ions, silver ion (Ag+) has attracted extensive attention because of its interesting characteristics and multi-functions, which allow it possible to apply to electricity [5], photography [6], decoration [7] and other fields [8–11]. On the other hand, glutathione (GSH) as an important regulatory substance in the cell, can participate in many important biochemical reactions in the body [12-15]. The variation of GSH concentration in internal environment is closely related to the developmental state of physiological disease such as cataract, AIDS and heart disease [16,17]. It is well known that Ag+ can strongly interact with GSH which possesses thiol functional group [18,19]. So, it is particularly meaningful to develop a simple analytical way to detect Ag+ and GSH sensitively and selectively in biological system.

Recently, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have drawn great interest because of their outstanding fluorescence (FL), low-toxicity and biocompatible characteristics [20-24], which make CDs the promising fluorescent probes with wide applications for sensing and biological imaging. For example, Chao Xia et al. synthesized ratio metric fluorescent CDs for pH nanoprobe [25]. Recently, Fushuang Niu et al. prepared N-doped CDs for detecting alkaline phosphatase [26]. Sada Venkateswarlu et al. prepared carbon nanodots for ultrasensitive detection of Hg2+ [27]. Our group also prepared N-doped CDs for monitoring Fe3+ and pH [28]. Simultaneously, as far as we know, the research of metal ions and thiol-containing amino acids has received widespread attentions. Zhu Han et al. prepared ratio metric CDs for the detection of copper ions and GSH [29]. Jiangjiang Gu et al. synthesized fluorescent probe for detecting Au(III) and GSH [19]. Compared with traditional analytical methods, such fluorescence “on-off-on” method does not need long time, complex operation and expensive equipment [30-32]. Hence, it is worth developing this promising application in the field of biomedicine.

Considering the importance of detecting Ag+ and biothiols, the fluorescence “on-off-on” modes are gradually established to detect Ag+ and biothiols sensitively in recent years. For example, Yuan Jiao et al. prepared ratiometric fluorescence CDs for the detection of Ag+ and GSH [33]. Ju Qin et al. reported CDs fluorescent sensor for “on-off-on” detection of Ag+ and GSH [34]. Sen Liao et al. proposed an “on-off-on” mode probes for Ag+ and cysteine (Cys) detection [35]. It is noted that in these researches, most CDs sensitively detection of Ag+ is based on FL quenching effect of metal ions. Herein, we designed a novel facile nanosensor to determine Ag+ by making use of the oxidative ability of Ag+ towards N-CDs prepared by solvothermal way. During the reaction, N-CDs play a role of reductant and stabilizer and Ag+ forms AgNPs by autocatalytic reaction. Distinct from the previously reported metal ions quenching fluorescence, the FL intensity of the N-CDs we obtained can be enhanced through the formation of AgNPs. Meanwhile, since thiols could form Ag-S bonds with Ag+ in solution, the redox reactions between Ag+ and N-CDs could be restrained when introduced GSH into the solution, preventing the FL of N-CDs being enhanced. Thus, biothiol detection also achieved in our experiment. The N-CDs solutions could return to nearly original fluorescence when introduced GSH. Thus, a fluorescence “off-on-off” nanosensor for Ag+ and GSH based on N-CDs was established. Furthermore, the N-CDs could be applied to multicolor bioimaging owning to their unique biocompatible nature.

P-phenylenediamine (PPD) and ethylenediamine (EDA) were purchased from Aladdin Ltd (Shanghai, China). NaOH and HCl were purchased from Beijing Chemical Works (Beijing, China). Silver nitrate (AgNO3) and other metal ion solutions were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Glutathione (GSH) and other amino acids were obtained from Macklin. The ultrapure water (18.25 MΩ cm) was used in the all experiment.

The N-CDs in our work were prepared using the method reported previously in our laboratory [36]. In brief, 0.1 g p-phenylenediamine was firstly dissolved with 10 mL ethylenediamine and then the mixture was agitated for 1 h. Subsequently, the homogeneous solution was transferred into the 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave chamber and reacted at 200 ℃ for 12 h. Then, 500 Da dialysis bag was used to purify the solution. Finally, the as-synthesized N-CDs solution was acquired and stored at 4 ℃ for further research and characterization.

Morphological feature and sizes of N-CDs were acquired using a JEOL JEM 2100F transmission electron microscope (TEM, Hitachi). The XPS data were measured on Thermo ESCALAB 250XI X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy. The FT-IR spectrum was recorded by a Bruker Tensor 27 ATR-FTIR spectrometer. The UV–vis spectra were measured using a Shimadzu UV-2600 spectrometer. The FL spectra were recorded with a Horiba Fluoromax-4 fluorescence spectrometer. Confocal fluorescence microscopy images were taken by confocal laser fluorescence microscopy (TCS SP5Ⅱ, Leica, Germany).

Hela cells (1 × 104) were placed in confocal culture dishes at 37 °C, incubated overnight in a 5% CO2 incubator. The next day, after the cells were attached, 40 μL N-CDs solution was added and the cells were incubated for 1.5 h. Finally, after washing three times with phosphate buffered saline (PBS), the cells were focused and observed on a confocal microscope by imaging software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence measurements for Ag+ and GSH
ce:section.ce:para: The detection of Ag+ and GSH was conducted as follows. Typically, AgNO3 solutions with the concentration ranging from 1 to 70 μmol were added into PBS buffer (pH 7.0, 5 mL) containing 20 μL N-CDs (0.1 mg/mL). Then, the mixture was heated in water bath at 50 ℃ for 30 min. The FL spectra measured at 400 nm excitation wavelength. For detecting GSH, GSH solutions at different concentration and Ag+ (100 μM) were introduced into N-CDs solutions. Subsequently, the N-CDs/Ag+/GSH solutions were incubated together in 50 ℃ water bath for 30 min. Finally, FL spectra were recorded. The selectivity of the N-CDs to Ag+ and GSH was tested via introducing different metal ions and amino acids into N-CDs solutions at the identical situations mentioned.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The N-CDs were prepared via a hydrothermal way, which has been mentioned by our previous report [36], using PPD and EDA as precursors. The resulted N-CDs were initially analyzed via TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 , the N-CDs were relatively uniform and isolated from each other with the average size of 3.93 nm. The FT-IR spectroscopy was performed to analyze functional groups of N-CDs. As depicted in Fig. S1, the absorption peak at 3439 cm−1 represents N–H stretching vibration, demonstrating the presence of amino groups on N-CDs surface. The band at 2947 cm−1 is ascribed to –CH2 stretching vibration. Besides, the N-CDs also include CC (1616 cm−1), C–O (1094 cm−1), C–N (1075 cm−1) chemical bonds. The XPS spectrum of the N-CDs was further executed for demonstrating the FT-IR results. The XPS spectra of N-CDs shown in Fig. S2a exhibits three peaks at 284.3 eV (C 1s), 398.9 eV (N 1s) and 531.2 eV (O 1s). The high-resolution of C1s spectrum (Fig. S2b) indicates the presence of three dominant peaks of CC/C–C (284.02 eV), C-N (284.53 eV) and –CONH (288.15 eV) [37]. The three peaks of 398.14 eV, 398.96 eV and 400.62 eV in the N1s spectra depicted in Fig. S2c could be related to pyridine-like N, amino N and pyrrole-like N, respectively [36,37], demonstrating the possession of nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface of N-CDs. As shown in Fig. S2d, two peaks at 531.3 eV and 532.15 eV in O1s spectra correspond to CO, C–O/–OH functional groups [38-40], respectively. The oxygen-rich groups make the N-CDs possess excellent water solubility, which is beneficial to detect materials in water. In view of the above analysis, the surface of N-CDs we prepared is rich in functional groups like –CO and N–H

The optical characteristics of N-CDs are shown in Fig. 2 . The N-CDs solution is light brown under sunlight and exhibits bright yellow fluorescence under UV light irradiation. Fig. 2a shows the intense absorption peak at 244 nm, which is ascribed to the π-π* transition of CC. Besides, a weak band at 298 nm is owing to the n-π* transition of C-N in N-CDs. The wavelength of the maximum emission of the N-CDs shifts from 548 to 580 nm when the excitation wavelength increases from 360 to 550 nm, demonstrating that the N-CDs exhibits excitation-dependent property (Fig. 2b). Besides, the N-CDs show excellent FL intensity when the excitation wavelength is 400 nm and the corresponding emission wavelength is 548 nm. Hence, 400 nm was selected as the optimal excitation wavelength in the following examinations. This excitation-dependent nature of the N-CDs allows them to act as multicolor cell imaging reagent. The reason for this phenomenon maybe related to broad particle range or the presence of various surface states on N-CDs [38,39,41]. After the N-CDs were successfully synthesized, the effects of pH and ionic strength on the optical stability of N-CDs were investigated (Fig. S3). Interestingly, the FL intensity of N-CDs exhibits irregular trends under broad range of pH from 2 to 14. The highest intensity is acquired at pH 7–8 demonstrating that N-CDs are potential candidate in biological applications. Besides, the FL intensity of N-CDs barely changed when the concentration of NaNO3 gradually increased to 2.0 M, which hints that N-CDs have excellent ionic anti-interference ability and have a broad application prospect in complex environments.

In view of the fluorescence “off-on-off” mode of N-CDs/Ag+/GSH system, N-CDs were used to detect Ag+ and GSH selectively and sensitively. In order to get the best sensing performance, the time-dependent FL intensity of N-CDs/Ag+ and N-CDs/Ag+/ GSH was inspected. As shown in Fig. S4, reaction time of N-CDs/Ag+ and N-CDs/Ag+/GSH was about 30 min under water bath conditions. As exhibited in Fig. 3 a, unlike the ordinary quenching phenomenon of metal ions on the fluorescence of N-CDs, the FL intensity of the N-CDs distinctly increased gradually when the concentration of the Ag+ varied from 0 to 70 μM. As depicted in Fig. 3b, the changes of the FL intensity showed good linear relationship to the concentration of Ag+ in the range of 0–70 μM. This linear correlation can be expressed as Y((I0 − I)/I0) = −0.0587X − 0.04485, which means a well-pleasing detection property in tracing Ag+ (Fig. 3c). The results of the detection of Ag+ were compared with the previous reported literatures in terms of detection limit and linear range (Table S1). The limit of detection (LOD) of N-CDs is calculated to be 0.091 μM, which is lower than the previously reported results. Meanwhile, the FL increasing phenomenon of N-CDs is shown in Fig. S5, the significant changes in color and fluorescence brightness can be observed under daylight and UV light.

The selectivity of the N-CDs for Ag+ was measured with the addition of different metal ions to the N-CDs solution (Fig. 3d). The metal ions (100 μM) were: Ni2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Ca2+, Ba2+ and Ag+. Ag+ can result in evident enhancement in FL intensity. Simultaneously, Fe3+ and Cu2+ can quench of the FL intensity of N-CDs in some extent, other metal ions have less effect on FL intensity of the N-CDs. The consequence manifests that the N-CDs can detect Ag+ with high sensitivity and selectivity by making use of its special enhancement fluorescence function to N-CDs.

Detection of GSH has been conducted based on the “off-on-off” FL model of the N-CDs. FL intensity of N-CDs gradually decreases with the increase of GSH concentration from 0 to 100 μM (Fig. 4 a). What’s more, the decrease of the FL intensity of the N-CDs exhibits excellent linear relationship as the GSH concentration increasing (Fig. 4b). A good linear equation (Y = 0.0048X + 0.09831) can be obtained within a range of 20–70 μM with the R2 value of 0.995 (Fig. 4c). The limit of detection (LOD) is 1.125 μM. Table S2 compared the GSH detection performance with other reported results. It is worth noting that the detection limit in this paper is comparable or superior to those reported methods. In addition, the interference examination shows that other amino acids possess negligible effects on the fluorescence of N-CDs (Fig. 4d), indicating the outstanding selectivity of the prepared N-CDs for GSH detecting. All these results confirm that the N-CDs can act as the highly sensitive fluorescence probes for detecting Ag+ and GSH.

To research the reasons why N-CDs possessed superior selectivity towards Ag+ and GSH, the reaction substances of N-CDs with Ag+ and GSH were characterized by TEM and UV methods. When Ag+ (70 μM) is added into N-CDs solution, it can be seen that the AgNPs with size of about 80 nm dispersed on a membrane layer (Fig. 5 a) which corresponds to the literature mentioned [42]. Meanwhile, the UV–Vis absorption in Fig. 5b appears surface plasma resonance (SPR) peak of AgNPs [43], and the SPR peak disappears when GSH is introduced into N-CDs/Ag+ system. Thus, a possible mechanism is proposed in order to explore why N-CDs exhibit interesting “off-on-off” fluorescence phenomena for Ag+ and GSH (Fig. 5c). It is well known that free electrons and photons on the metal nanoparticles surface are coupled to each other to form localized surface plasmons (LSPs) when light irradiates metal nanoparticles. If the frequency of the incident light coincides with the frequency of the free electron oscillation, the surface electrons will oscillate greatly, resulting in a localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) phenomenon, which will cause the local electromagnetic field around the metal nanostructures enhancement [44,45]. Based on these theories, we assumed that a redox reaction occurs on the surface of the N-CDs when Ag+ is added to the N-CDs solution. As the reaction continued, Ag+ was reduced to metal Ag and formed silver nanoparticles (AgNPs), while the N-CDs were oxidized. The thus-formed AgNPs can catalyze the reaction between N-CDs and Ag+. The AgNPs formed and N-CDs cannot be separated, which further affects the excitation and emission processes of fluorescence resulting in enhanced fluorescence. Simultaneously, it is well known that there is strong binding ability between Ag+ and thiols. Therefore, after adding GSH into the solutions, Ag+ can be competitively taken away by GSH which prevents the formation of AgNPs and the fluorescence of N-CDs returns to original intensity again. In addition, as shown in Fig. 4a and 5c, with the increase of GSH concentration, the competitive destruction of AgNPs will result in more N-CDs collision opportunities. Since N-CDs have distinct sp2 skeletons, more collision opportunities will cause a slight red-shift in the FL emission spectra.

The N-CDs can also be applied to multicolor fluorescent imaging in HeLa cells based on their excellent feature of low-toxic and biocompatibility. Multicolor cellular imaging is necessary because multiple colorful certifications can prevent judgment errors due to the automatic brightness of the creature. As shown in Fig. 6 , after HeLa cells were cultivated with N-CDs solutions at 37 °C for 1.5 h, the N-CDs could permeate HeLa cells and exhibited intense fluorescence in the cells. What is more, the HeLa cells show blue (Fig. 6b), yellow (Fig. 6c), red (Fig. 6d) fluorescence under excitation wavelength at 410 nm, 500 nm, and 600 nm. The bright-field (Fig. 6a) of these Hela cells subjected to N-CDs treatments exhibits that morphology is hardly affected. It hints that N-CDs are biocompatible and low toxicity to Hela cells. Furthermore, the merged images show that the N-CDs possess excellent cell membrane permeability (Fig. 6e–g). These results indicate that N-CDs could be used as multicolor cellular agents for bioimaging.

In summary, the N-CDs were prepared as an effective “off-on-off” fluorescent sensor for detecting Ag+ and GSH. As far as we know, there are few reports that metal ions possess fluorescence-enhancing effect on CDs. In this work, N-CDs can directly reduce Ag+ to form AgNPs without introducing reducing agent or stabilizer. As a result, the fluorescence of N-CDs is enhanced by the formation of AgNPs. When GSH is added, the fluorescence of the N-CDs restores to nearly original strength. It can be ascribed to the strong binding interaction between Ag+ and GSH, preventing the reaction of N-CDs and Ag+. Additionally, N-CDs could be applied to multicolor bioimaging, thus possessing promising prospects in biological field. We think that prepared N-CDs could offer an effective method for searching cheap, facile and highly sensitive and selective sensors in analytical areas.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by A Project Subsidized the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (1107047002).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2021.106156.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots-based fluorescent probe for “off-on” sensing of Hg(II) and I−

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. He, H. Zhang, J. Zou, Y. Liu, J. Zhuang, Y. Xiao, B. Lei, Carbon dots-based fluorescent probe for “off-on” sensing of Hg(II) and I−, Biosens. Bioelectron. 79 (2016) 531-535.

Mahboob, Biomarkers of oxidative stress in rat for assessing toxicological effects of heavy metal pollution in river water, Environ. Sci. Pollut. R. 22 (2015) 13453-13463.

Chen, Study of heavy metal pollution, ecological risk and source apportionment in the surface water and sediments of the Jiangsu coastal region, China: A case study of the Sheyang Estuary, Mar. Pollut. Bull. 137 (2018) 601-609.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent carbon quantum dot hydrogels for direct determination of silver ions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Cayuela, M.L. Soriano, S.R. Kennedy, J.W. Steed, M. Valcarcel, Fluorescent carbon quantum dot hydrogels for direct determination of silver ions, Talanta 151 (2016) 100-105.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly sensitive and selective detection of silver ions and silver nanoparticles in aqueous solution using an oligonucleotide-based fluorogenic probe

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.-H. Lin, W.-L. Tseng, Highly sensitive and selective detection of silver ions and silver nanoparticles in aqueous solution using an oligonucleotide-based fluorogenic probe, Chem. Commun. 43 (2009) 6619-6621.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One-pot synthesis of carbon nanodots for fluorescence turn-on detection of Ag+ based on the Ag+-induced enhancement of fluorescence

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.H. Gao, Y.Z. Lu, R.Z. Zhang, S.J. He, J. Ju, M.M. Liu, L. Li, W. Chen, One-pot synthesis of carbon nanodots for fluorescence turn-on detection of Ag+ based on the Ag+-induced enhancement of fluorescence, J. Mater. Chem. C 3 (2015) 2302-2309.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: N-dots as a photoluminescent probe for the rapid and selective detection of Hg2+ and Ag+ in aqueous solution

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Wu, M. Feng, X. Chen, X. Tang, N-dots as a photoluminescent probe for the rapid and selective detection of Hg2+ and Ag+in aqueous solution, J. Mater. Chem. B 4 (2016) 2086-2089.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile synthesis of sulfur-doped graphene quantum dots as fluorescent sensing probes for Ag+ ions detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Bian, C. Shen, Y. Qian, J. Liu, F. Xi, X. Dong, Facile synthesis of sulfur-doped graphene quantum dots as fluorescent sensing probes for Ag+ ions detection, Sens. Actuators B Chem. 242 (2017) 231-237.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: In vitro cytotoxity of silver: implication for clinial wound care

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: V.K. Poon, A. Burd, In vitro cytotoxity of silver: implication for clinial wound care, Burns 30 (2004) 140-147.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Exposure-related health effects of silver and silver compounds: a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P.L. Drake, K.J. Hazelwood, Exposure-related health effects of silver and silver compounds: a review, Ann. Occup. Hyg. 49 (2005) 575-585.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly selective detection of glutathione using a quantum-dot-based off-on fluorescent probe

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.F. Lin, C.Y. Bao, X.H. Zhong, C.C. Zhao, L.Y. Zhu, Highly selective detection of glutathione using a quantum-dot-based off-on fluorescent probe, Chem. Commun. 46 (2010) 2971-2973.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent progress in fluorescent and colorimetric chemosensors for detection of amino acids

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Zhou, J. Yoon, Recent progress in fluorescent and colorimetric chemosensors for detection of amino acids, Chem. Soc. Rev. 41 (2012) 52-67.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A simple fluorescent off-on probe for the discrimination of cysteine from glutathione

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.H. Gao, X.H. Li, L.H. Li, J. Zhou, H.M. Ma, A simple fluorescent off-on probe for the discrimination of cysteine from glutathione, Chem. Commun. 51 (2015) 9388-9390.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A dual emission fluorescent probe enables simultaneous detection of glutathione and cysteine/homocysteine

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.F. Yang, Q. Huang, Y.G. Zhong, Z. Li, H. Li, M. Lowry, T.O. Escobedo, R.M. Strongin, A dual emission fluorescent probe enables simultaneous detection of glutathione and cysteine/homocysteine, Chem. Sci. 5 (2014) 2177-2813.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Coumarin-based turn-on fluorescence probe for specific detection of glutathione over cysteine and homocysteine

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L.W. He, Q.Y. Xu, Y. Liu, H.P. Wei, Y.H. Tang, W.Y. Lin, Coumarin-based turn-on fluorescence probe for specific detection of glutathione over cysteine and homocysteine, ACS. Appl. Mater. Interfaces 7 (2015) 12809-12813.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An aryl-thioether substituted nitrobenzothiadiazole probe for the selective detection of cysteine and homocysteine

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dot cluster as an efficient “off-on” fluorescent probe to detect Au (III) and glutathione
